## GPS Geodesy

In a nutshell…

The **Global Positioning System** (GPS) is a space geodetic technique used for positioning and navigation, based on ranging measurements with a a constellation of 24+ satellites that broadcast a radio signal on two frequencies in the L-band. Using antennas setup on well-monumented benchmark and dual-frequency GPS receivers, it is possible to convert the satellite-antenna range measurements into positions with a millimeter-level precision. Consequently, repeated measurements of a network of sites that covers a region of active deformation provides a direct measurements of displacements and strain rates in the region.

![GPS 1slide 1](../_Media/gps_1slide_1_med.png)

**Repeated episodic measurements** of a network of survey sites is the classical approach for monitoring crustal deformation. The GPS technique permits the measurement of a large number of geodetic sites within a short time, typically 2 to 3 weeks (a GPS "campaign"). This strategy provides the displacement field over a region of active deformation with a dense enough spatial sampling to detect deformation gradients and short wavelength variations in the strain pattern, which are critical data to understand the physical processes controlling deformation and the hazard associated with active faults.

**Continuous measurements** at permanent GPS sites are an alternate strategy for high-precision GPS positioning. They provide continuous time series of the site positions, critical for the study of transient deformation. In the case of an earthquake, in particular, continuous GPS measurements provide unambiguous measurements of coseismic and postseismic deformation. Coseismic motions are critical to understand the seismic rupture characteristics, while postseismic motion provide key information on viscous relaxation processes in the lithosphere.

![GPS 1slide 2](../_Media/gps_1slide_2_med.png)

**Applications of GPS measurements** in geosciences abound. Measuring secular tectonic deformation comes to mind first, from the global or regional motions of ridig plates or blocks, to the accumulation of elastic strain at plate boundary faults. GPS measurements can also capture transient deformation signals, caused for instance by seasonal loading of the earth crust, postseismic deformation, or volcano-magmatic processes.

![GPS 1slide 3](../_Media/gps_1slide_3_med.png)

# **What is geodesy?**

## Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding the Earth's **geometric shape**, **orientation in space**, and **gravity field**.

![Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding three fundamental properites of the Earth](/facts/geodesy.jpg)

*Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding three fundamental properites of the Earth.*

Many organizations use geodesy to map the U.S. shoreline, determine land boundaries, and improve transportation and navigation safety. To measure points on the Earth’s surface, geodesists assign coordinates (similar to a unique address) to points all over the Earth. In the past, geodesists determined the coordinates of points by using Earth-based surveying tools to measure the distances between points. Today, geodesists use space-based tools like the Global Positioning System (GPS) to measure points on the Earth’s surface.

Geodesists must accurately define the coordinates of points on the surface of the Earth in a consistent manner. A set of accurately measured points is the basis for the National Spatial Reference System, which allows different kinds of maps to be consistent with one another.

To measure the Earth, geodesists build simple mathematical models of the Earth which capture the largest, most obvious features. Geodesists have adopted the ellipsoid as the most basic model of the Earth. Because the ellipsoid is based on a very simple mathematical model, it can be completely smooth and does not include any mountains or valleys. When additional detail of the Earth is needed, geodesists use the [geoid](/facts/geoid.html). A geoid has a shape very similar to global mean sea level, but this exists over the whole globe, not just over the oceans.

A modern instrument for geodetic [measurements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Measurements "Measurements") using [satellites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNSS "GNSS")

**Geodesy** or **geodetics**[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-1) is the [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science") of measuring and representing the [geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), [gravity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth "Gravity of Earth"), and [spatial orientation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") of the [Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth "Earth") in [temporally varying](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_change "Relative change")[3D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space"). It is called [planetary geodesy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planetary_geodesy "Planetary geodesy") when studying other [astronomical bodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_body "Astronomical body"), such as [planets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planet "Planet") or [circumplanetary systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-VK-2)

[Geodynamical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodynamics "Geodynamics") phenomena, including [crustal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") motion, [tides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tide "Tide"), and [polar motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion"), can be studied by designing global and national [control networks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodetic_control_network "Geodetic control network"), applying [space geodesy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_geodesy "Space geodesy") and terrestrial geodetic techniques, and relying on [datums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") and [coordinate systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system").

Geodetic job titles include **geodesist** and **geodetic surveyor**.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-3)

History
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Geodesy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of geodesy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_geodesy "History of geodesy")

Geodesy began in pre-scientific [antiquity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), so the very word geodesy comes from the [Ancient Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") word γεωδαισία or _geodaisia_ (literally, "division of Earth").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-routledge-4)

Early ideas about the figure of the Earth held the Earth to be [flat]

*   Topics Toggle submenu
    
    [Data Pathfinders by Topic](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics "Data Pathfinders by Topic")
    
    [![Image 5: An icon depicting Atmosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/atmosphere_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/atmosphere "Atmosphere")
    
    [Atmosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/atmosphere "Atmosphere")
    
    The atmosphere is a gaseous envelope surrounding and protecting our planet from the intense radiation of the Sun.
    
    [![Image 6: An icon depicting Biosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/biosphere_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/biosphere "Biosphere")
    
    [Biosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/biosphere "Biosphere")
    
    The biosphere encompasses all life on Earth and extends from root systems to mountaintops and all depths of the ocean.
    
    [![Image 7: An icon depicting Climate Indicators](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/climate_indicators_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/climate-indicators "Climate Indicators")
    
    [Climate Indicators](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/climate-indicators "Climate Indicators")
    
    Climate Indicators hold key information for the most relevant domains of climate change: temperature and energy, atmospheric composition, ocean and water as well as the cryosphere.
    
    [![Image 8: An icon depicting Cryosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/cryosphere_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/cryosphere "Cryosphere")
    
    [Cryosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/cryosphere "Cryosphere")
    
    The cryosphere encompasses the frozen parts of Earth, including glaciers and ice sheets, sea ice, and any other frozen body of water.
    
    [![Image 9: An icon depicting Human Dimensions](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/human_dimensions_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/human-dimensions "Human Dimensions")
    
    [Human Dimensions](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/human-dimensions "Human Dimensions")
    
    The human dimensions discipline includes ways humans interact with the environment and how these interactions impact Earth’s systems.
    
    [![Image 10: An icon depicting Land Surface](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/land_surface_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/land-surface "Land Surface")
    
    [Land Surface](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/land-surface "Land Surface")
    
    The land surface discipline includes research into areas such as shrinking forests, warming land, and eroding soils.
    
    [![Image 11: An icon depicting Ocean](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/ocean_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/ocean "Ocean")
    
    [Ocean](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/ocean "Ocean")
    
    This vast, critical reservoir supports a diversity of life and helps regulate Earth’s climate.
    
    [![Image 12: An icon depicting Solid Earth](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/solid_earth_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/solid-earth "Solid Earth")
    
    [Solid Earth](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/solid-earth "Solid Earth")
    
    Processes occurring deep within Earth constantly are shaping landforms.
    
    [![Image 13: An icon depicting Sun-Earth Interactions](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/sun_earth_interactions_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/sun-earth-interactions "Sun-Earth Interactions")
    
    [Sun-Earth Interactions](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/sun-earth-interactions "Sun-Earth Interactions")
    
    The Sun influences physical and chemical processes in Earth’s atmosphere.
    
    [![Image 14: An icon depicting Terrestrial Hydrosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/modules/custom/project/earthdata_blocks/images/static_menu_blocks/terrestrial_hydrosphere_icon.png)](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/terrestrial-hydrosphere "Terrestrial Hydrosphere")
    
    [Terrestrial Hydrosphere](https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/terrestrial-hydrosphere "Terrestrial Hydrosphere")
    
    The terrestrial hydrosphere includes water on the land surface and underground in the form of lakes, rivers, and groundwater along with total water storage.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Geodesy underpins GPS technology, providing accurate measurements of Earth's shape and gravitational variations, essential for precise positioning and navigation.
Geodesy enhances Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) by establishing reference frames and ensuring the accuracy of positioning data across various applications.
Geodesy integrates with satellite systems, facilitating real-time data collection and analysis, enabling advancements in navigation, environmental monitoring, and disaster management.
